subject: Physics
title: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

questions.text: The minimum energy required by an electron to escape from a metal surface is called:
questions.explanation: Work function is the minimum energy needed to liberate an electron from the metal surface.

questions.text: The phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metal surface when light of suitable frequency falls on it is:
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the photoelectric effect, first explained by Einstein.

questions.text: Which of the following does NOT affect the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons?
questions.explanation: Max K.E. depends on frequency, not intensity. Intensity determines the number of photoelectrons (current).

questions.text: According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, Kmax is equal to:
questions.explanation: Einstein's equation is hν = Φ + Kmax, hence Kmax = hν - Φ.

questions.text: The threshold frequency is the frequency below which:
questions.explanation: If the incident frequency is less than the threshold frequency, no electrons are emitted regardless of intensity.

questions.text: The stopping potential is a measure of:
questions.explanation: eV₀ = Kmax. Stopping potential directly measures the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons.

questions.text: In photoelectric effect, the photocurrent is directly proportional to:
questions.explanation: Intensity represents the number of photons per unit area; more photons result in more emitted electrons.

questions.text: What happens to the stopping potential if the intensity of incident light is doubled?
questions.explanation: Stopping potential depends on the frequency of light, not its intensity.

questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength λ of a particle of mass m and velocity v is:
questions.explanation: λ = h/p = h/mv. This represents the wave nature of matter.

questions.text: The momentum of a photon of frequency ν is:
questions.explanation: p = E/c = hν/c.

questions.text: Which experiment confirmed the wave nature of electrons?
questions.explanation: The Davisson-Germer experiment observed electron diffraction, proving matter waves exist.

questions.text: The rest mass of a photon is:
questions.explanation: Photons travel at the speed of light and have zero rest mass.

questions.text: If a proton and an electron have the same K.E., which has a shorter de Broglie wavelength?
questions.explanation: λ = h / √(2mK). Since mass of proton > mass of electron, λ of proton is shorter.

questions.text: The slope of the graph between stopping potential (V₀) and frequency (ν) is:
questions.explanation: V₀ = (h/e)ν - (Φ/e). The slope of V₀ vs ν is h/e.

questions.text: A photon of wavelength 662 nm has momentum (h = 6.62 x 10⁻³⁴ Js):
The energy of a photon of wavelength λ is:
questions.explanation: E = hν and ν = c/λ, so E = hc/λ.

questions.text: If the frequency of incident light is increased, the stopping potential:
questions.explanation: Higher frequency means higher maximum K.E. of electrons, requiring more potential to stop them.

questions.text: Matter waves are:
questions.explanation: Matter waves are probability waves and do not require a medium nor consist of electric/magnetic fields.

questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential V is approximately:
questions.explanation: The formula is λ = √(150/V) Å ≈ 12.27/√V Å.

questions.text: A photocell converts:
questions.explanation: Photocells use the photoelectric effect to generate electric current from light.

questions.text: Light behaves as a particle when it:
questions.explanation: Photoelectric effect and Compton effect can only be explained by the particle (photon) model of light.

questions.text: The unit of Planck's constant is same as that of:
questions.explanation: Both have units of Js (kg m² s⁻¹).

questions.text: According to de Broglie, a moving particle is associated with a wave. This wave is:
questions.explanation: De Broglie waves are often called pilot waves that guide the particle.

questions.text: The work function of a metal depends on:
questions.explanation: Work function is a characteristic property of the material.

questions.text: If light of wavelength λ₁ and λ₂ (λ₁ > λ₂) is incident on a metal, which has a higher stopping potential?
questions.explanation: Shorter wavelength (λ₂) means higher frequency and thus higher stopping potential.

questions.text: The time lag between incidence of light and emission of photoelectrons is of the order of:
questions.explanation: Photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.

questions.text: Number of photoelectrons emitted per second is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Intensity determines the number of photons hitting the surface per second.

questions.text: The energy of a photon in eV for λ in Å is given by E ≈:
questions.explanation: E = hc/λ. Using constants, E(eV) ≈ 12400 / λ (Å).

questions.text: Which of the following particles moving with same velocity has longest de Broglie wavelength?
questions.explanation: λ = h/mv. For same velocity, the particle with smallest mass (electron) has longest wavelength.

questions.text: The K.E. of an electron with de Broglie wavelength λ is:
questions.explanation: λ = h/√(2mK) => λ² = h²/(2mK) => K = h²/(2mλ²).

questions.text: If E is the energy of a photon, its momentum is:
questions.explanation: From E=mc² and p=mc, p = E/c.

questions.text: The variation of photocurrent with intensity for a fixed frequency is a:
questions.explanation: Photocurrent is directly proportional to intensity.

questions.text: Cathode rays are made of:
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson discovered that cathode rays are streams of fast-moving electrons.

questions.text: If the threshold wavelength for a metal is 5000 Å, light of which wavelength will NOT cause emission?
questions.explanation: Emission occurs only if λ ≤ λ₀. Since 6000 > 5000, no emission occurs.

questions.text: Planck's constant has dimensions of:
questions.explanation: E = hν => h = E/ν = [ML²T⁻²]/[T⁻¹] = [ML²T⁻¹].

questions.text: In a Davisson-Germer experiment, the maximum intensity is observed at a scattering angle of:
questions.explanation: The maximum was observed at 50° for an accelerating voltage of 54V.

questions.text: An electron, an alpha particle, and a proton have the same momentum. Which has the highest K.E.?
questions.explanation: K = p²/2m. For constant p, smallest mass (electron) gives highest K.E.

questions.text: If a metal has work function 2 eV and light of 5 eV is incident, the stopping potential is:
questions.explanation: eV₀ = hν - Φ = 5 - 2 = 3 eV. So V₀ = 3 V.

questions.text: The wave nature of light explains:
questions.explanation: Interference and diffraction require wave theory, whereas the others require particle theory.

questions.text: The concept of 'quanta' of energy was introduced by:
questions.explanation: Max Planck proposed that energy is radiated or absorbed in discrete packets called quanta.
